column1:
Why should a segmental pressure exam be performed with the patient supine?

A: To increase the pressure in the venous system and reduce the pressures in the arterial system
B: To provide the most comfortable position for the patient during the lengthy exam
C: To eliminate the influence of hydrostatic pressure on the brachial pressures
D: To eliminate the influence of hydrostatic pressure on the ankle pressures
column2:
Answer: D. To eliminate the influence of hydrostatic pressure on the ankle pressures

Feedback:
Hydrostatic pressure can cause an overestimation of pressures in the ankles. Segmental pressure evaluations should be
performed with the patient supine.

column1:
The following pressures were obtained in a patient with left claudication. What are the ABI values for both legs? Right: PTA 128mmHg, DPA, 124mmHg, Calf 130mmHg, Thigh 140mmHg, Brachial 130mmHg Left: PTA 98mmHg, DPA 122mmHg, Calf 126mmHg, Thigh 138mmHg, Brachial 126mmHg

A: RT 0.95, LT 0.75
B: RT 1.01, LT 0.96
C: RT 0.98, LT 0.77
D: RT 0.98, LT 0.94
column2:
Answer: D. RT 0.98, LT 0.94

Feedback:
Right 128mmHg/130mmHg = 0.98, Left 122mmHg/130mmHg = 0.94
You should not have complicated math problems on the registry exam. The most important part of any calculation is to know
how to set up the calculation. The highest calf artery pressure is divided by the highest brachial pressure to calculate the ABI.

column1:
The pre and post exercise pressure information displayed from the right leg demonstrates which of the following?

A: Single level stenosis
B: Multiple level stenosis
C: Raynaud's Syndrome
D: Normal study
column2:
Answer: A. Single level stenosis

Feedback:
Single Level Obstruction-pressures return to normal 2-6 minutes post exercise
Multi Level Obstruction-pressures return to normal 6-12 minutes post exercise

column1:
Which of the following describes the expected change in the PVR tracing on an immediate post procedure evaluation of a patient after successful revascularization of the common femoral artery?

A: increased amplitude by >100%
B: loss of the dicrotic notch
C: increased amplitude by >50%
D: decreased amplitude by >50%
column2:
Answer: C. increased amplitude by >50%

Feedback:
Normal PVR tracings from an extremity should be high amplitude with a dicrotic notch. If the revascularization was a success,
the amplitude of the waveform should have increased at least 50% compared to the amplitude on the pre-op exam.

column1:
A diabetic patient presents for a segmental pressure exam. The patient complains of an injury to his big toe that won't seem to heal and has been open for over 3 weeks. The digital pressure in the affected toe will most likely be _________.

A: 30-60mmHg
B: 60-90mmHg
C: <30mmHg
D: >200mmHg
column2:
Answer: C. <30mmHg

Feedback:
Toe pressures <30mmHg correlate with a non-healing wound.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes cuff placement for a segmental pressure exam?

A: Once the patient is supine, ask him/her to raise their foot about 6 inches above the table to you can properly wrap the cuff around the leg
B: The proximal edge of the cuff should always cover a bony structure like the malleolus or tibial tubercle
C: The width of the cuff should be at least 20% narrower than the girth of the limb segment
D: Cuffs should be placed straight around the limb, rather than angled to the curve of the limb
column2:
Answer: D. Cuffs should be placed straight around the limb, rather than angled to the curve of the limb

Feedback:
The bladder of the cuff must be placed over the artery being evaluated and only compress soft tissue, not bony structures. The
below-knee or upper calf cuff should be placed just distal to the tibial tubercle or pressure readings will be falsely elevated. The
lower calf cuff should be placed with the distal edge 2-3cm above the medial malleolus. The width of the cuff should be at least
20% wider than the girth of the limb segment. The patient should rest the leg during cuff placement. Raising the leg causes
muscle contraction and when released, the cuff will be too loose. Cuffs should be placed straight around the limb, rather than
angled to the curve of the limb.

column1:
A patient complains of left arm numbness and pain that increases in severity when it is cold outside. An upper extremity arterial duplex exam demonstrates a>50% stenosis in the distal subclavian artery. The PPG exam demonstrates additional decrease in digital flow with cold exposure. What explains these findings?

A: Thoracic Outlet Syndrome
B: Primary Raynaud Syndrome
C: Buerger Disease
D: Secondary Raynaud Phenomenon
column2:
Answer: D. Secondary Raynaud Phenomenon

Feedback:
Changes in flow patterns in the hand due to the application of external stimuli (cold) are a characteristic sign of Raynaud
Disease. Secondary Raynaud Phenomenon refers to these changes that occur in conjunction with a diseased arterial system in
the upper extremity. Primary Raynaud Syndrome refers to these changes that occur in conjunction with a non-diseased arterial
system in the upper extremity.

column1:
When performing a PVR exam of the lower extremity arteries, how are the waveforms obtained?

A: Starting at the ankle on the right leg, proceed up through the right thigh before repeating the process with the left leg
B: Starting at the right thigh cuff and moving toward the right ankle cuff, then repeating the process with the left leg
C: Starting at both thighs and moving down through the cuffs on both legs simultaneously
D: Starting at both ankles and moving up through the cuffs on both legs simultaneously
column2:
Answer: C. Starting at both thighs and moving down through the cuffs on both legs simultaneously

Feedback:
A segmental pressure exam is performed on one leg at a time, while a PVR exam is performed on both legs simultaneously.
Segmental pressures are taken at the ankle first and then move up the leg. PVR exams are performed from the thighs down to
the ankle.

column1:
If the blood pressure in the ankle is obtained with the patient's legs in the dependent position:

A: the pressure will be underestimated
B: it will be the same as if the patient is evaluated in the supine position
C: the pressure will be falsely elevated
D: the ankle will demonstrate the same response as if an exercise exam was just performed
column2:
Answer: C. the pressure will be falsely elevated

Feedback:
If the blood pressure in the ankle is obtained with the patient's legs in the dependent position, the pressure will be falsely
elevated due to the effects of hydrostatic pressure. Extremity arterial exams should be performed with the patient supine to
eliminate the effects of hydrostatic pressure.

column1:
An asymptomatic patient demonstrates a normal left ABI, but the left tibial arteries demonstrate a monophasic waveform with sharp upstroke and rapid deceleration. What is the most likely cause for this finding?

A: Scale settings set too high to detect low velocity flow reversal.
B: Filter settings set too low to detect diastolic flow reversal.
C: The patient should be evaluated for popliteal entrapment.
D: Raynaud syndrome
column2:
Answer: A. Scale settings set too high to detect low velocity flow reversal.

Feedback:
The rapid acceleration and deceleration indicate a high resistance waveform that would be normal in the tibial arteries. The
diastolic flow reversal component can be very small due to low velocity and a short duration of the flow. Decreased scale and
filter settings will aid in visualization of this component of the waveform.

column1:
An arterial duplex exam demonstrates monophasic flow in the popliteal and calf arteries. The bilateral ABI calculations are over 1.0. What type of testing can be used to better evaluate the pressures in the lower extremities in this patient?

A: Repeat the ABI using larger width of cuff to reduce the peak pressure obtained
B: Repeat the ABI using smaller width of cuff to reduce the peak pressure obtained
C: Impedance plethysmography
D: Digit plethysmography
column2:
Answer: D. Digit plethysmography

Feedback:
Digital plethysmography can be performed to evaluate flow and systolic pressure measurements of the toes. If the ABI is normal
and the Doppler evaluation does not correspond with the ABI, digital tracings and pressures should be obtained to clarify the
findings. Medial calcification can result in a normal ABI with monophasic flow in the calf arteries. The digital arteries are not
typically affected by medial calcification.

column1:
Which of the following is NOT normally performed after the injection of papaverine into the tissues proximal to the shaft of the penis:

A: Measure the diameter of the cavernous arteries
B: Repeat the ABI measurements
C: Doppler waveforms are obtained in the cavernous arteries and veins 1-2 minutes post-injection
D: Apply the 2.5cm blood pressure cuff around the shaft of the penis
column2:
Answer: B. Repeat the ABI measurements

Feedback:
When performing a penile Doppler exam:
Obtain Doppler samples of the CFA, cavernous arteries and veins in the penis.
Doppler samples will also be taken in the PTA and DPA while performing ankle pressures.
The ABI should be evaluated to determine if PAD can be causing the erectile dysfunction.
The diameter of the cavernous arteries should be measured.
After the injection the penile artery diameters and velocities are repeated.
The penile pressure is obtained using a 2.5cm cuff and the PBI is calculated.
The ABI is performed before the injection to assess any PAD that may be present.

column1:
Abnormal results for a penile Doppler evaluation that suggest impotence include _______________ peak systolic flow velocity and a peak pressure ____________.

A: <30cm/s, <60mmHg
B: <60cm/s, >60mmHg
C: >60cm/s, >30mmHg
D: >30cm/s, >60mmHg
column2:
Answer: A. <30cm/s, <60mmHg

Feedback:
Abnormal results for a penile Doppler evaluation that suggest impotence include <30cm/s peak systolic flow velocity and a peak
pressure <60mmHg.

column1:
When a patient performs exercise on a treadmill what will be the normal change in arterial flow in the legs?

A: unchanged flow pattern
B: increased peripheral resistance
C: increased antegrade flow in diastole
D: decreased antegrade flow in diastole
column2:
Answer: C. increased antegrade flow in diastole

Feedback:
When the patient exercises, the leg muscles require more blood flow for proper operation. The resistance will drop at the ankle
to allow the increased flow in diastole.

column1:
The difference in systolic pressure between two adjacent levels in the same leg should be no more than ___________mmHg.

A: 15
B: 20
C: 25
D: 30
column2:
Answer: D. 30

Feedback:
A drop in pressure of 30mmHg or more between segments indicates disease between the two segments.

column1:
When performing a treadmill test for PAD, exercise is normally performed ________________. When performing a reactive hyperemia exam for PAD, the cuffs are inflated ________________.

A: with the patient standing, with the patient supine
B: for up to 5 minutes, for up to 5 minutes
C: with the patient supine, with the patient standing
D: for up to 10 minutes, for up to 5 minutes
column2:
Answer: B. for up to 5 minutes, for up to 5 minutes

Feedback:
When performing a treadmill test for PAD, exercise is normally performed for up to 5 minutes. When performing a reactive
hyperemia exam for PAD, the cuffs are inflated for up to 5 minutes. Reactive hyperemia is used to simulate exercise in patients
that cannot perform exercise. If the patient experiences symptoms, the exam can be terminated earlier.

column1:
Which of the following screening techniques for peripheral arterial disease can distinguish between a stenosis and an occlusion in a vessel?

A: Segmental pressure evaluations
B: Doppler evaluations
C: Pulse Volume Recording
D: Photoplethysmography
column2:
Answer: B. Doppler evaluations

Feedback:
Segmental pressure and PVR exams can only detect the decrease in flow volume to the legs at a general location in the leg. The
results do not provide information on specific vessels, nor can they distinguish stenosis from occlusion.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes normal results for an air plethysmography (APG) exam?

A: low ejection fraction, low residual venous fraction and venous filling index
B: high ejection fraction, with low residual venous fraction and venous filling index
C: high ejection fraction and residual venous fraction with low venous filling index
D: low ejection fraction and low residual venous fraction with high venous filling index
column2:
Answer: B. high ejection fraction, with low residual venous fraction and venous filling index

Feedback:
Most blood should be ejected out of the leg with calf contraction (high EF%)
The VFI is the rate of venous filling. Slower fill rates indicate normal veins. If the VFI is small, that indicates reflux refilling the
veins quickly.
Residual Venous Fraction is the % of venous volume remaining after tiptoe exercises. Low RVF% of blood should remain in the
leg with calf contraction. Higher RVF% values indicate reflux occurred during exercise.

column1:
A patient presents for a bilateral segmental pressure exam and complains of mild leg pain since their heart attack last year. A duplex exam showed medium velocity, triphasic flow with minimal atherosclerosis in the bilateral arteries. The recorded pressures are as follows: Right Arm 110mmHg, Right PTA 88mmHg, Right DPA 92mmHg Left Arm 105mmHg, Left PTA 90mmHg, Left DPA 94mmHg Which of the following could explain the findings?

A: The normal arterial duplex exam correlates with the normal lower extremity segmental exam with right ABI 1.20 and left ABI 1.17.
B: The patient is most likely suffering from Buerger disease.
C: The patient most likely has reduced cardiac output causing the right ABI 0.84 and left ABI 0.85.
D: The patient most likely has reduced cardiac output causing the right ABI 0.8 and left ABI 0.82.
column2:
Answer: C. The patient most likely has reduced cardiac output causing the right ABI 0.84 and left ABI 0.85.

Feedback:
Reduced cardiac function with heart failure will lead to reduced pressures distally. Right ABI = 92 / 110 = 0.84; Left ABI = 94 / 110
= 0.85; always use the highest ankle pressure on each leg and use the higher of the two brachial pressures.

column1:
A segmental pressure exam is performed with the following results. Lt arm 140mmHg Lt ankle 155mmHg Rt arm 110mmHg Rt ankle 145mmHg Which of the following correctly describes the findings?

A: There is no vascular disease present in the right leg, but the left leg demonstrates mild disease.
B: There is mild vascular disease present in the lower extremities.
C: There is mild vascular disease present in the lower extremities and there is most likely a subclavian steal present.
D: There is no vascular disease present in the legs, but there is most likely a subclavian steal present.
column2:
Answer: D. There is no vascular disease present in the legs, but there is most likely a subclavian steal present.

Feedback:
The ankle pressures are normal at a level higher than the highest brachial pressure. The large difference (>20mmHg) between
the brachial pressures is usually indicative of a subclavian steal.

column1:
A double peaked waveform obtained during PPG evaluation of the digits of the left hand is indicative of:

A: Raynaud syndrome
B: Subclavian stenosis
C: Thoracic outlet syndrome
D: palmar arch stenosis
column2:
Answer: A. Raynaud syndrome

Feedback:
The double peaked PPG tracing is indicative of vasospasm seen with Raynaud syndrome.

column1:
A normal lower extremity arterial response to reactive hyperemia is:

A: <50% decrease in ankle pressures that return to baseline within one minute
B: <50% increase in ankle pressures with return to baseline in under 5 minutes
C: <35% decrease in ankle pressures that return to baseline within one minute
D: no change in ankle pressures
column2:
Answer: C. <35% decrease in ankle pressures that return to baseline within one minute

Feedback:
A normal lower extremity arterial response to reactive hyperemia is <35% drop in ankle pressures that return to normal within 1
minute. By cutting off arterial inflow for a few minutes, the extremity becomes "starved" for oxygenated blood. When the cuff is
released the inflow should return to baseline levels quickly if there is no disease present to stop the inflow.

column1:
Which of the following statements is true regarding a PPG exam for venous insufficiency?

A: Prolonged venous refill time (>20s) indicates competent venous valves.
B: Tourniquets are used in conjunction with PPG exam to depict the extent of DVT.
C: The best readings with severe insufficiency will be obtained at an ulceration site.
D: Waveforms obtained for a venous PPG exam are similar to those obtained in a venous duplex exam.
column2:
Answer: A. Prolonged venous refill time (>20s) indicates competent venous valves.

Feedback:
PPG sensors must be placed on areas of intact skin. Tourniquets are used in conjunction with PPG exam to depict the extent of
venous insufficiency. Longer VRT values are desired, short refill times indicate reflux of venous outflow.

column1:
An upper extremity segmental pressure exam produces the following pressures: Brachial RT 140mmHg AND LT 132mmHg Radial RT 144mmHg AND LT 136mmHg Ulnar RT 138mmHg AND LT 138mmHg 2nd Digit RT 130mmHg AND LT 104mmHg What should you do next for this patient?

A: end the exam and report right palmar arch disease
B: perform digital pressures on at least one more digit on the left hand
C: end the exam and report left palmar arch disease
D: perform digital pressures on at least one more digit on the right hand
column2:
Answer: B. perform digital pressures on at least one more digit on the left hand

Feedback:
When the arm pressures are normal but the digital pressure is abnormally reduced, there could be disease in the palmar arch
or the single digit. To provide a complete evaluation, at least one more digit on the left hand should be evaluated. If the
additional digit pressure is abnormal, disease in the palmar arch is the most likely cause for the reduced digit pressures. If the
additional digit pressure is normal, the remaining digits should be tested to confirm that the reduced pressure is limited to the
single digit and disease of that digital artery should be suspected.

column1:
Most commonly the posterior tibial and dorsalis pedis arteries are used to obtain the pressure at the ankle. Which of the following describes the correct calculation of the ankle brachial index?

A: highest brachial pressure divided by the highest ankle pressure on each ankle
B: highest right ankle pressure divided by the right brachial pressure and highest left ankle pressure divided by the left brachial pressure
C: lowest ankle pressure on each leg divided by the highest brachial pressure
D: highest ankle pressure on each leg divided by the highest brachial pressure
column2:
Answer: D. highest ankle pressure on each leg divided by the highest brachial pressure

Feedback:
When calculating the ABI, the highest ankle pressure on each leg is divided by the highest brachial pressure.

column1:
A 25yr old presents with recent onset of left calf pain when riding his bike. He has been training for a race for the last six months without problem until 2 weeks ago. Exercise testing demonstrates a 50mmHg drop in pressure compared to the right leg with no drop in pressure. These findings are most suggestive of:

A: Popliteal entrapment
B: Buerger disease
C: Popliteal cyst
D: Incorrect cuff placement
column2:
Answer: A. Popliteal entrapment

Feedback:
As the patient trained for the race, muscle built up in the calf over last six months to cause problems now. Popliteal entrapment
is the most likely cause for the symptoms.

column1:
When performing a photoplethysmography exam for arterial insufficiency, where should you place the sensor?

A: dorsal aspect of the great toe
B: medial distal calf
C: groin
D: distal pad of the great toe
column2:
Answer: D. distal pad of the great toe

Feedback:
Venous Insufficiency is assessed by placing the sensor on the distal calf. Arterial disease is assessed by placing the sensor on the
pads of the toes.

column1:
Which of the following exam techniques would be recommended to provide additional information related to the findings on the image?

A: pulse volume recording
B: ascending venography
C: segmental pressures
D: photoplethysmography
column2:
Answer: D. photoplethysmography

Feedback:
The image demonstrates significant venous reflux. Segmental pressures and PVR waveforms aid in arterial disease evaluation.
Venography can be helpful but only when the contrast is injected at the groin for the descending venography exam. PPG
tracings can be used to assess and quantify venous reflux.

column1:
When using the rapid cuff inflator to evaluate the great saphenous vein in the distal thigh, where should you place the pneumatic cuff?

A: distal thigh
B: ankle
C: foot/metatarsals
D: upper calf
column2:
Answer: D. upper calf

Feedback:
When evaluating a patient for insufficiency, a rapid cuff inflator can be used for a consistent augmentation of each vessel. The
cuff is usually placed just distal to the area of interest. 70 to 80 mm Hg of pressure is applied quickly, held for a few seconds,
and released quickly. Reversal of flow is assessed after release of the augmentation compression. Veins distal to the cuff can
also be evaluated using proximal augmentation. Flow reversal would be documented during the compression part of the
augmentation.
30
Which drawing demonstrates an abnormal result for a venous

column1:
Which drawing demonstrates an abnormal result for a venous photoplethysmography exam?

A: A
B: B
C: Both are abnormal responses
D: Unable to determine without the application of a tourniquet on the thigh
column2:
Answer: B. B

Feedback:
The exam involves placing a light sensor on the distal calf. Multiple calf raises are performed to empty the blood from the calf.
The venous refill time of the calf is measured. A venous refill time less than 20 seconds indicates venous reflux. Image B shows a
VRT <20sec.

column1:
A patient presents with a history of HTN, diabetes and claudication. The arterial Doppler evaluation demonstrates monophasic flow in all calf vessels but the ABI values are LT 1.36 and RT 1.32. Which of the following correctly describes the findings?

A: The exam is normal and additional testing should be performed to rule out extrinsic vessel compression due to median arcuate ligament syndrome.
B: The exam is normal and additional testing should be performed to rule out extrinsic vessel compression due to popliteal entrapment syndrome.
C: The most likely diagnosis is thromboangiitis obliterans, which leads to medial calcification of the calf vessels.
D: The ABI values are abnormally elevated due to a diabetic complication called medial calcification.
column2:
Answer: D. The ABI values are abnormally elevated due to a diabetic complication called medial calcification.

Feedback:
Monophasic flow in the extremities is normally accompanied with an ABI less than 0.9. Diabetes can lead to medial calcification
of the arterial walls which will falsely elevate the ankle pressures.

column1:
You obtain the PPG tracing from the second digit of the left hand. Which of the following is NOT an expected cause for the findings?

A: Thromboangiitis obliterans
B: Thoracic outlet syndrome
C: Raynaud disease
D: Leriche syndrome
column2:
Answer: D. Leriche syndrome

Feedback:
Leriche syndrome refers to stenosis or occlusion of the distal aorta. This would lead to a similar PPG tracing from the LOWER
extremity digits.

column1:
It is important to wait at least one minute before repeating what measurement?

A: MCA mean velocity
B: brachial pressure on an arm with a hemodialysis graft
C: volume flow in a hemodialysis graft
D: ankle blood pressure
column2:
Answer: D. ankle blood pressure

Feedback:
When you are obtaining a blood pressure for a second time, it is important to wait at least 1 minute between cuff inflations.
Pressure results may be inaccurate if the system is not given the opportunity to normalize after the first pressure measurement.
NEVER perform a blood pressure assessment on am arm with a hemodialysis graft.

column1:
When evaluating arterial pressures in the toes, the patient should be:

A: standing
B: walking
C: supine
D: sitting
column2:
Answer: C. supine

Feedback:
Arterial pressures should be evaluated with the patient supine to eliminate the effects of hydrostatic pressure. All types of
resting extremity arterial testing should be performed with the patient supine.

column1:
Which of the following is a reason to perform exercise with a segmental pressure exam?

A: to differentiate an SFA stenosis from an occlusion
B: to evaluate volume changes in the limb
C: to differentiate true claudication from pseudoclaudication
D: to evaluate the effects of vasoconstriction
column2:
Answer: C. to differentiate true claudication from pseudoclaudication

Feedback:
True claudication will be associated with a decrease in ABI with exercise. Pseudoclaudication will be associated with no change
in the ABI with exercise.

column1:
The Adson maneuver is helpful in the diagnosis of what vascular disorder?

A: Thoracic outlet syndrome
B: Venous insufficiency
C: Raynaud syndrome
D: Collateral formation with carotid occlusion
column2:
Answer: A. Thoracic outlet syndrome

Feedback:
The Adson maneuver is a patient position used to evaluate flow changes with thoracic outlet syndrome. Palpate the radial pulse
on the affected side with the elbow fully extended. Have the patient rotate their head to the side being tested and extend the
neck. Then abduct, extend, and laterally rotate the shoulder. From this position, have the patient take a deep breath and hold.
Assess the pulse response. A positive test is a decrease in pulse vigor from the starting position to the final position.

column1:
A segmental pressure exam demonstrates the following pressures; distal thigh 144, calf 166, ankle 132. Which of the following could explain these results?

A: medial calcification or venous insufficiency
B: improper cuff placement and incorrect cuff size, but not medial calcification
C: incorrect cuff size would be the only possible cause
D: incorrect cuff size, medial calcification and improper cuff placement
column2:
Answer: D. incorrect cuff size, medial calcification and improper cuff placement

Feedback:
The calf pressure should not demonstrate an increased pressure related to the thigh pressure. All of the choices can lead to an
overestimated pressure measurement.

column1:
If the systolic brachial pressure is 116mmHg, what is the minimum toe pressure that would be considered normal?

A: 80mmHg
B: 60mmHg
C: 100mmHg
D: 70mmHg
column2:
Answer: D. 70mmHg

Feedback:
The normal pressure in the lower extremity digits is normally at least 60% of the brachial pressure. 116 x 0.60 = 70mmHg. The
toe pressure is considered normal if it is 70mmHg or higher.

column1:
An upper extremity segmental pressure exam produces the following pressures: Brachial RT 140mmHg AND LT 132mmHg Radial RT 144mmHg AND LT 136mmHg Ulnar RT 138mmHg AND LT 138mmHg 2nd Digit RT 130mmHg AND LT 104mmHg Which of the following correctly describes how to report the findings?

A: Suspected disease of the proximal left ulnar artery
B: Suspected disease of the right palmar arch or digital artery of the second digit
C: Suspected disease of the proximal left radial artery
D: Suspected disease of the left palmar arch or digital artery of the second digit
column2:
Answer: D. Suspected disease of the left palmar arch or digital artery of the second digit

Feedback:
When the arm pressures are normal but the digital pressure is abnormally reduced, there could be disease in the palmar arch
or the single digit. To provide a complete evaluation, at least one more digit on the left hand should be evaluated. If the
additional digit pressure is abnormal, disease in the palmar arch is the most likely cause for the reduced digit pressures. If the
additional digit pressure is normal, the remaining digits should be tested to confirm that the reduced pressure is limited to the
single digit and disease of that digital artery should be suspected.

column1:
Which method is preferred to evaluate suspected popliteal entrapment?

A: PPG tracing of the digits at rest and during dorsiflexion
B: PPG tracing of the digits at rest and during treadmill exercise
C: pulse volume recordings of the leg arteries with the patient supine and standing
D: segmental pressures before and after treadmill exercise
column2:
Answer: A. PPG tracing of the digits at rest and during dorsiflexion

Feedback:
Evaluation of a patient with suspected popliteal entrapment should be performed using PPG sensors to record changes in flow
during calf contraction. Active plantar or dorsiflexion of the foot can be performed to cause controlled calf muscle contraction.

column1:
When performing post-occlusive reactive hyperemia, pressure measurements in the lower extremities are obtained:

A: immediately after cuff release and every 2 minutes until pressures return to baseline levels
B: 1 minute after cuff release and every 30 seconds until pressures return to baseline levels
C: 1 minute after cuff release and every 2 minutes until pressures return to baseline levels
D: immediately after cuff release and every 30 seconds until pressures return to baseline levels
column2:
Answer: D. immediately after cuff release and every 30 seconds until pressures return to baseline levels

Feedback:
When performing post-occlusive reactive hyperemia of the lower extremities, pressure measurements are obtained
immediately after cuff release and every 30 seconds until pressures return to baseline levels. Recovery time is shorter for
reactive hyperemia than treadmill testing.

column1:
When evaluating suspected thoracic outlet syndrome, a PPG exam can be performed with the sensor placed on the _________________ or a Doppler exam can be performed with the transducer placed _______________________.

A: 2nd digit, on the radial artery
B: 1st digit, on the carotid artery
C: lateral wrist, on the brachial artery
D: medial wrist, on the radial artery
column2:
Answer: A. 2nd digit, on the radial artery

Feedback:
When evaluating suspected thoracic outlet syndrome, a PPG exam can be performed with the sensor placed on the 2nd digit or
a Doppler exam can be performed with the transducer placed on the radial artery.

column1:
Which of the following patients would benefit from an exercise segmental pressure exam?

A: Patient with rest pain and resting ABI 0.4
B: Patient with COPD and resting ABI of 0.9
C: Patient with claudication and a resting ABI of 0.95
D: Patient with claudication and resting ABI 0.6
column2:
Answer: C. Patient with claudication and a resting ABI of 0.95

Feedback:
A normal resting ABI can become abnormal with exercise in patients with less severe disease. The patient with COPD would
most likely be recommended for reactive hyperemia. An ABI that is less than 0.9 demonstrates PAD is present. The exercise
exam will not provide any additional information to assist on the diagnosis of these patients.

column1:
When performing a segmental pressure exam on a patient with claudication, which of the following describes the response to exercise in a patient with pseudoclaudication?

A: Leg pain will not be associated with a drop in ankle pressure
B: ABI will most likely be 0.5-0.9
C: Leg pain will be associated with a drop in ankle pressure
D: ABI will most likely be <0.5
column2:
Answer: A. Leg pain will not be associated with a drop in ankle pressure

Feedback:
Pseudoclaudication has an orthopedic or neurogenic cause and is not caused by ischemia. The ABI will be normal and there will
be no drop in ankle pressure with exercise.

column1:
Which blood vessel is most commonly evaluated with Doppler to obtain the thigh pressure when performing a segmental pressure exam?

A: popliteal artery just above the calf cuff
B: distal calf artery with lowest ankle pressure
C: distal calf artery with highest ankle pressure
D: femoral artery just proximal to the thigh cuff
column2:
Answer: C. distal calf artery with highest ankle pressure

Feedback:
The PTA or DPA that yields the highest pressure at the ankle should be used to obtain the pressures for the remaining arterial
segments. The Doppler signal from the calf arteries are used for ALL pressure measurements in the leg.

column1:
Lower extremity arterial segmental pressure exam results in a post exercise basal pressure recovery time of 2 to 6 minutes which suggests:

A: Iliac Disease
B: Multi-level stenosis
C: Single level stenosis
D: Thoracic outlet syndrome
column2:
Answer: C. Single level stenosis

Feedback:
Lower extremity arterial segmental pressure exam resulting in a post exercise basal pressure recovery time of 2 to 6 minutes
suggests single level disease. Lower extremity arterial doppler exam resulting in a post exercise basal pressure recovery time
greater than 6 minutes suggests multi-level disease.

column1:
When performing a lower extremity venous Doppler for insufficiency,

A: the intensity of the Doppler signal indicates the severity of the reflux
B: augmentation pressure is applied quickly, held for at least 15 seconds, and released quickly
C: a minimum of 30 seconds between augmentation maneuvers is recommended
D: the GSV should be evaluated with patient bearing weight on the leg
column2:
Answer: C. a minimum of 30 seconds between augmentation maneuvers is recommended

Feedback:
When performing a lower extremity venous Doppler for insufficiency, a minimum of 30 seconds between augmentation
maneuvers is recommended. The veins need sufficient time to refill before augmentation is repeated.

column1:
A patient presents for penile Doppler and segmental pressure exam due to impotence. The ABI is >1.0 bilaterally. The PBI is 0.8. What should be done next to evaluate this patient?

A: Use papaverine to assess for increased venous outflow
B: Write the results as normal with no explanation of impotence
C: Use papaverine to assess for decreased venous outflow
D: Have the patient perform 5 minutes of exercise on the treadmill at 12% grade
column2:
Answer: A. Use papaverine to assess for increased venous outflow

Feedback:
If the PBI is normal in the initial exam, papaverine can be injected at the base of the penis. If the PBI drops, vasculogenic
impotence should be suspected. If venous outflow increases post-injection, this can be another cause of impotence in cases
with normal arterial inflow.

column1:
Rest pain is associated with ankle pressures less than ______________ and toe pressures less than _____________ will be associated with a non- healing wound.

A: 70mmHg, 50mmHg
B: 50mmHg, 50mmHg
C: 100mmHg, 30mmHg
D: 50mmHg, 30mmHg
column2:
Answer: D. 50mmHg, 30mmHg

Feedback:
Rest pain is associated with ankle pressures less than 50mmHg and toe pressures less than 30mmHg will be associated with a
non-healing wound.

column1:
An ABI of 0.77 indicates ________________ disease.

A: unable to determine without exercise portion
B: mild - moderate
C: severe
D: minimal
column2:
Answer: B. mild - moderate

Feedback:
ABI Criteria
Minimal disease 0.9 - 1.0
Mild/Moderate disease 0.5 - 0.9
Severe disease <0.5

column1:
Pulse volume recording:

A: is used to assess arterial insufficiency and DVT
B: is commonly performed with a segmental pressure exam
C: will demonstrate waveforms that are nearly identical to the analog Doppler waveforms in a normal patient
D: readings should be taken beginning with the distal cuff and moving proximally
column2:
Answer: B. is commonly performed with a segmental pressure exam

Feedback:
Pulse volume recording is used to assess arterial disease. PVR exams are usually performed with segmental pressure exams.
Reading are taken first in the proximal extremity and then moving distally to the next cuff. The normal waveform will be
monophasic with rapid upstroke, sharp systolic peak, prolonged downstroke and prominent reflection (dicrotic notch). Normal
Doppler tracings would be triphasic.

column1:
While performing an arterial Duplex exam, you obtain a triphasic waveform in the proximal femoral artery but the rest of the leg arteries demonstrate a monophasic waveform. The brachial pressure is 140mmHg. Which of the following lists the correct expected pressures from the four cuff segmental exam on the same patient?

A: Upper Thigh 155mmg, Distal Thigh 155mmHg, Popliteal 120mmHg, PTA 110mmHg
B: Upper Thigh 155mmg, Distal Thigh 125mmHg, Popliteal 160mmHg, PTA 155mmHg
C: Upper Thigh 175mmg, Distal Thigh 120mmHg, Popliteal 110mmHg, PTA 95mmHg
D: Upper Thigh 120mmg, Distal Thigh 120mmHg, Popliteal 110mmHg, PTA 100mmHg
column2:
Answer: C. Upper Thigh 175mmg, Distal Thigh 120mmHg, Popliteal 110mmHg, PTA 95mmHg

Feedback:
A drop in pressure >30mmHg between two adjacent segments indicates significant disease in the artery between the two cuffs.
If there is a suspected mid SFA stenosis or occlusion, there will be a significant drop in pressure between the two thigh cuffs.

column1:
An abnormal Penile/Brachial Index will be:

A: < 1.0
B: > 1.0
C: < 0.65
D: > 0.65
column2:
Answer: C. < 0.65

Feedback:
If the penile blood pressure is less than 65% of the systemic pressure, vasculogenic impotence is diagnosed. A normal PBI is
greater than or equal to 0.75. If the PBI is between 0.65 and 0.75, it is considered only a marginal reduction and vasculogenic
cause for erectile dysfunction cannot be diagnosed..

column1:
A segmental pressure exam is performed for a patient who is experiencing rest pain in the right leg. An ABI of 0.42 was found on the right leg. Which of the following statements is true regarding the findings?

A: The patient will be referred to a vascular surgeon for intervention.
B: Exercise testing will provide additional diagnostic information for this patient.
C: A PVR exam will provide additional diagnostic information for this patient.
D: A tourniquet will be applied to the upper thigh and the ankle pressure repeated.
column2:
Answer: A. The patient will be referred to a vascular surgeon for intervention.

Feedback:
There is significant disease present. Exercise testing or a pulse volume recording exam will not provide additional information
that will aid the physician in diagnosis and treatment. The patient is already a candidate for surgical intervention.

column1:
A patient presents for an exercise arterial exam after an abnormal resting exam. The report from the resting exam describes: elevated velocities that indicate a stenosis of >50% in the distal femoral artery on the left leg, biphasic waveforms in the PTA and DPA and an ABI of 0.96. There is no change in the waveforms or decrease in ankle pressures in the left leg with exercise. What could explain these findings?

A: obstructed venous outflow
B: Raynaud syndrome
C: congestive heart failure
D: extensive collateral formation
column2:
Answer: D. extensive collateral formation

Feedback:
Exercise induces peripheral vasodilation. If collateral flow is present, it will compensate for the expected drop in flow and
pressure from a diseased vessel.

column1:
Flow velocity in a lower extremity arterial bypass graft is considered abnormal once it falls below __________.

A: 40cm/s
B: 60cm/s
C: 30cm/s
D: 50cm/s
column2:
Answer: A. 40cm/s

Feedback:
If the flow velocity in a lower extremity arterial bypass graft falls below 40cm/s, graft failure is suspected.

column1:
How should you prepare the patient to start the examination used to assess the venous refill time using photoplethysmography?

A: Supine legs elevated with sensor at the medial malleolus
B: Seated at the edge of the table with lower legs dangling, sensor placed at medial malleolus
C: Standing with the sensor at the medial malleolus
D: Supine legs elevated with pressure cuff on thigh
column2:
Answer: B. Seated at the edge of the table with lower legs dangling, sensor placed at medial malleolus

Feedback:
A PPG exam is used to calculate the venous refill time (VRT). A sensor is placed at the medial malleolus and the patient is seated
at the edge of the table with their legs dangling. The patient performs a series of foot dorsiflexions to empty the blood from the
calf. The strip chart recorder demonstrates the changes in blood volume over time. The time that it takes for the line to plateau
again is the venous refill time. VRT >20 seconds is normal; <20 seconds indicates insufficiency.
If using APG to evaluate the VRT, a cuff is placed on the lower leg and the patient is put in the supine position with the affected
leg elevated. Once the flow empties from the leg, the patient stands up and the VRT is recorded as volume returns to maximum
levels under the cuff.

column1:
Which of the following is not proper procedure for a venous PPG evaluation of the legs?

A: The optimal speed for the strip chart recorder is 5mm/sec
B: Patient is seated with legs dangling over edge of bed for the exam
C: A tourniquet is applied to the leg after initial testing shows a VRT < 20 sec
D: Always place the sensor over the largest varicose vein in the calf
column2:
Answer: D. Always place the sensor over the largest varicose vein in the calf

Feedback:
Patient is seated with legs dangling over edge of bed for the exam. A sensor is placed on the distal medial lower leg. Never place
the PPG sensor on an open wound, ulcer or varicose vein. A strip chart recorder documents flow changes. The optimal speed
for the strip chart recorder is 5mm/sec. A tourniquet is applied to the leg after initial testing shows a VRT < 20 sec.

column1:
When a patient performs exercise on a treadmill what will be the normal change in the ankle brachial indices?

A: decreased by less than 0.3
B: unchanged or mild increase
C: unchanged or mild decrease
D: increased by more than 0.3
column2:
Answer: B. unchanged or mild increase

Feedback:
The pressure in the ankles and arms may demonstrate a mild increase in pressure with exercise but the brachial and ankle
pressure will increase together in a normal patient. This will lead to a consistent ABI calculation. A mild increase in the ABI may
also be normal. A decrease in ankle pressure indicates an abnormal response.

column1:
A 9 year old presents with chronic fatigue and decreased palpable femoral pulses. The ankle brachial index is performed with the following results. Lt arm 168mmHg Lt ankle 95mmHg Rt arm 174mmHg Rt ankle 86mmHg; Which of the following is the most likely explanation of the findings?

A: There is most likely a coarctation of the descending aorta.
B: There is most likely atherosclerotic vascular disease present in both lower extremities.
C: There is most likely a subclavian steal present.
D: The bilateral ABIs are normal.
column2:
Answer: A. There is most likely a coarctation of the descending aorta.

Feedback:
The arm pressures are significantly higher than normal. The leg pressures are lower than normal. The bilateral occurrence
indicates an aortic abnormality. A coarctation typically occurs just distal to the left subclavian origin. This causes increased
pressures above the coarctation/obstruction = elevated brachial pressures. Pressures will drop distal to the
coarctation/obstruction = lower ankle pressures bilaterally.

column1:
What is the most accurate method of thigh pressure assessment?

A: 4 cuff segmental pressure exam
B: Doppler velocity used in the Bernoulli equation to calculate thigh pressure
C: 3 cuff segmental pressure exam
D: Intravenous catheter measures pressure in the superficial femoral vein
column2:
Answer: C. 3 cuff segmental pressure exam

Feedback:
The 4 cuff method uses two smaller cuffs to obtain two separate thigh pressures. These cuffs are usually smaller than the thigh
cuff used in the 3 cuff method. Smaller cuffs tend to overestimate the thigh pressure. The larger cuff used in the 3 cuff method
provides a more accurate pressure.

column1:
What is the primary purpose of using a tourniquet during a venous PPG exam of the leg?

A: to determine if thrombus is present in the deep or superficial system
B: to evaluate the perforators for reflux
C: to determine if thrombus is confined to the superficial system
D: to determine if venous insufficiency is confined to the superficial system
column2:
Answer: D. to determine if venous insufficiency is confined to the superficial system

Feedback:
When the venous refill time (VRT) is less than 20 seconds, a tourniquet is applied to the leg at the groin to occlude superficial
venous flow. The VRT is re-evaluated and if it is now >20 seconds, the insufficiency is only present in the superficial system. If
the VRT does not return to normal, the insufficiency is in the deep system (and possibly the superficial system).

column1:
Which of the following is a characteristic of a normal lower extremity arterial PVR waveform?

A: slow downslope
B: low amplitude
C: slow upslope
D: prominent dicrotic notch
column2:
Answer: D. prominent dicrotic notch

Feedback:
An abnormal PVR waveform will demonstrate a loss of the dicrotic notch, slow upslope, slow downslope and little difference
between systolic and diastolic blood volumes.

column1:
A segmental pressure exam demonstrates the following pressures; right arm: 136mmHg right leg: thigh 110mmHg, calf 100mmHg, ankle 92mmHg. Which of the following could explain these results?

A: aortic disease or popliteal disease
B: common iliac disease or distal femoral artery
C: medial calcification
D: aortic disease or common iliac disease
column2:
Answer: D. aortic disease or common iliac disease

Feedback:
The pressures in the entire leg are decreased which indicates proximal disease. Using the three cuff method, the brachial
pressure and thigh pressure are usually relatively the same. If the left leg is affected also, aortic disease is the most likely
diagnosis. If only the right leg is affected, then the cause is related to common iliac disease.

column1:
What can be done to inhibit light from the exam room from causing artifact on an upper extremity PPG tracing?

A: perform the exam with all lights off and windows covered
B: increase the room temperature
C: place acoustic gel between the PPG sensor and the skin
D: cover the patient's hand with a towel or sheet
column2:
Answer: D. cover the patient's hand with a towel or sheet

Feedback:
To reduce light from the exam room from causing artifact on the PPG tracing, cover the patient's hand with a towel or sheet.

column1:
A 65yr old male presents with mild left calf pain after walking 10 blocks. The pain is relieved by sitting and resting. On a resting arterial exam with ABI, the left ABI is 1.04. These findings are most suggestive of:

A: The venous system should be evaluated for thrombosis.
B: The venous system should be evaluated for possible insufficiency.
C: An exercise arterial should be performed to re-evaluate the ABI of the left leg before a diagnosis is made.
D: The patient is most likely experiencing an orthopedic or neurogenic problem and an MRI may be warranted.
column2:
Answer: C. An exercise arterial should be performed to re-evaluate the ABI of the left leg before a diagnosis is made.

Feedback:
Because the symptoms occur when the patient is walking, the leg should be evaluated with exercise to completely rule out a
vascular diagnosis.

column1:
A patient is short of breath after walking to the facility for the arterial duplex evaluation. What is the first thing you should do when starting his exam?

A: Have him rest for 20-30 minutes to allow flow in the legs to normalize
B: Take his brachial pressures.
C: Take his ankle pressures using the PTA and DPA.
D: Have him lie down and start with the Duplex exam and perform the pressures later.
column2:
Answer: A. Have him rest for 20-30 minutes to allow flow in the legs to normalize

Feedback:
If the patient does not rest, the exercise from his long walk can affect the results of the expected resting arterial exam. This can
lead to overestimation of the severity of the disease identified on the resting exam.

column1:
What is the most likely cause of the findings on the image?

A: bilateral peroneal artery occlusion
B: bilateral popliteal entrapment
C: bilateral iliac stenosis
D: embolism
column2:
Answer: D. embolism

Feedback:
Normal ABIs with abnormal digit tracings indicates obstruction of the digital arteries by embolisms. The patient should be
evaluated for AAA for a source for the emboli. Popliteal entrapment requires maneuvers during the exams and the calf arteries
and digital arteries would be affected. Bilateral iliac stenosis would cause abnormal waveforms in all areas in both legs.

column1:
A patient presents with left leg claudication. The right ABI is 1.32 and the left ABI is 1.37. What should you do next for this patient?

A: stop the segmental pressure exam and obtain toe pressures along with PVR waveforms
B: use the rapid cuff inflator to repeat the ankle pressure measurements
C: switch to 16cm thigh cuffs and continue the segmental exam to include the proximal calf and thigh pressures
D: end the exam and record the findings
column2:
Answer: A. stop the segmental pressure exam and obtain toe pressures along with PVR waveforms

Feedback:
When the ABI exceeds 1.3, there is most likely medial calcification present. Because medial calcification does not usually affect
the smaller digital arteries, the toe pressures can be obtained. PVR waveforms can be obtained to locate the obstruction, if the
TBI is abnormal.

column1:
Lower extremity arterial segmental pressure exam results in a post exercise basal pressure recovery time of > 6 minutes which suggests:

A: Popliteal entrapment syndrome
B: Single level stenosis
C: Multi-level stenosis
D: Iliac disease
column2:
Answer: C. Multi-level stenosis

Feedback:
Lower extremity arterial segmental pressure exam resulting in a post exercise basal pressure recovery time of 2 to 6 minutes
suggests single level disease. Lower extremity arterial doppler exam resulting in a post exercise basal pressure recovery time
greater than 6 minutes suggests multi-level disease.

column1:
When performing a photoplethysmography exam for venous insufficiency, where should you place the sensor?

A: dorsal aspect of the great toe
B: medial distal calf
C: distal pad of the great toe
D: groin
column2:
Answer: B. medial distal calf

Feedback:
Venous Insufficiency is assessed by placing the sensor on the distal calf. Arterial disease is assessed by placing the sensor on the
pads of the toes.

column1:
The following pressures were obtained in a patient during a segmental pressure exam. Where is the disease, if any? Right: PTA 136mmHg, DPA, 130mmHg, Calf 136mmHg, Low Thigh 144mmHg, High Thigh 160mmHg, Brachial 130mmHg Left: PTA 98mmHg, DPA 85mmHg, Calf 100mmHg, Low Thigh 114mmHg, High Thigh 128mmHg, Brachial 126mmHg

A: normal left leg, medial calcification right leg
B: aortic disease or left iliac disease
C: iliac disease left leg, normal right leg
D: left calf artery disease, normal right leg
column2:
Answer: C. iliac disease left leg, normal right leg

Feedback:
The left ABI value is reduced at 0.75. When using 4 cuffs, the high thigh pressure is normally 30-40mmHg higher than the
brachial pressure. The other pressures in the leg do not demonstrate a significant drop between consecutive segments. There is
iliac disease that is causing the decrease in the pressures in the entire leg. If aortic disease was present, both legs would be
affected.

column1:
A patient presents for a bilateral segmental pressure exam of the lower extremities. The chart lists a history of a bilateral mastectomy in 1980. How will this affect your exam?

A: The patient must be evaluated in the standing position for the pressure measurements
B: The pressure indices cannot be calculated and the exam will be limited to waveform analysis only
C: If the surgical procedure was performed more than 15 years ago, the exam should be completed using the standard protocol
D: A wrist pressure must be obtained instead of a brachial pressure and this number will be used to calculate the pressure indices
column2:
Answer: B. The pressure indices cannot be calculated and the exam will be limited to waveform analysis only

Feedback:
Brachial blood pressures are prohibited on the ipsilateral arm of a mastectomy patient. With the bilateral mastectomy, neither
brachial pressure should be obtained. If you cannot perform a brachial pressure or both brachial pressures are abnormal,
calculation of indices (ABI or TBI) would underestimate the disease. In these cases interpretation would need to be by waveform
analysis only.

column1:
A diabetic patient presents for a segmental pressure exam. The calf and ankle vessels are non-compressible with up to 220mmHg. What alternative exam can be performed to evaluate lower extremity pressures?

A: Doppler ultrasound
B: photoplethysmography and toe cuffs
C: photoplethysmography with ankle cuffs
D: pulse volume recording exam
column2:
Answer: B. photoplethysmography and toe cuffs

Feedback:
The tiny vessels of the toes are usually spared from medial calcification. PPG waveforms and pressure measurements can be
taken on the toes when the ankle pressures are falsely elevated.

column1:
When performing an upper extremity segmental pressure exam, you obtain the following readings on the right arm. Right upper arm 140mmHg Right radial artery 110mmHg Right ulnar artery 130mmHg. Which of the following correctly explains the findings?

A: radial artery disease
B: radial and ulnar artery disease
C: distal brachial artery obstruction
D: subclavian artery obstruction
column2:
Answer: A. radial artery disease

Feedback:
There is a 30mmHg drop in pressure between the upper arm and the radial artery at the forearm. This indicates radial artery
disease. The ulnar artery pressure is only mildly decreased and does not indicate disease. A brachial obstruction would affect
the pressure in both of the forearm arteries.

column1:
A patient presents with left lower extremity pain that awakens him at night. The pain is relieved by sitting at the side of the bed for a few minutes. What would the predicted ABI value be for the left leg?

A: 0.8
B: 0.6
C: 0.4
D: above 1.0; these symptoms are most likely unrelated to arterial disease
column2:
Answer: C. 0.4

Feedback:
The patient is experiencing rest pain which is found in patients with an ABI less than 0.5

column1:
A diabetic patient presents for extremity arterial evaluation with a rest pain bilaterally and blackened tips of the 4th and 5th toe on the left foot Right Ankle 148mmHg Left Ankle 144mmHg Highest brachial pressure 140mmHg. Which statement is true regarding these findings?

A: Toe pressures should be obtained for further evaluation.
B: The signs and symptoms are most likely related to dehydration and poor hygiene.
C: An exercise arterial exam is necessary to properly diagnose the patient.
D: The signs and symptoms are most likely related to associated venous insufficiency.
column2:
Answer: A. Toe pressures should be obtained for further evaluation.

Feedback:
Medial calcification does not always cause an excessive ABI measurement of greater than 1.30. The symptoms of significant
ischemia do not fit the normal ABI measurements. The diabetic history also adds to the risk for the medial calcification. Toe
pressures and/or PPG or PVR evaluation will provide better information on this patient.

column1:
Which of the following would cause the systolic pressure reading obtained from the below knee cuff to be falsely elevated?

A: cuff is too large
B: reading obtained with the patient supine
C: cuff placement over the tibial tubercle
D: the patient suffers from congestive heart failure
column2:
Answer: C. cuff placement over the tibial tubercle

Feedback:
The bladder of the cuff must be placed over the artery being evaluated and only compress soft tissue, not bony structures. The
below-knee or upper calf cuff should be placed just distal to the tibial tubercle or pressure readings will be falsely elevated.
Other causes of falsely elevated pressure readings include medial calcification, cuff too loose, cuff too small, patient not supine
(hydrostatic pressure).

column1:
Which of the following is a major limitation of a lower extremity segmental pressure exam when compared to a Doppler evaluation?

A: The segmental pressure technique is only valid for evaluating obstructive disease above the knee.
B: The segmental pressure technique is only valid for evaluating obstructive disease below the knee.
C: The segmental pressure technique cannot differentiate external iliac from common femoral stenosis/occlusion.
D: The segmental pressure technique cannot evaluate obstructive disease in the feet.
column2:
Answer: C. The segmental pressure technique cannot differentiate external iliac from common femoral stenosis/occlusion.

Feedback:
Segmental pressures can demonstrate the level of the problem in the extremity but cannot determine the extent of stenosis or
obstruction that is present. An abnormal thigh pressure indicates proximal disease. Without Doppler evaluation, there is no way
to differentiate if the obstruction is in the iliac or common femoral artery. The same is true with a pressure drop between the
distal thigh cuff and proximal calf cuff. Without US evaluation, there is no way to determine if the obstruction is in the popliteal
artery, tibioperoneal trunk or proximal calf artery, A drop in pressure between segments usually indicates disease, but further
investigation is necessary to determine if a stenosis or an occlusion is causing the drop in pressure. Collateral vessels in the legs
may falsely elevate pressures even during arterial obstruction. Ultrasound is necessary to determine the location and extent of
the disease.

column1:
Which of the following disorders would demonstrate normal digital pressures in the upper extremity?

A: Buerger disease
B: Thoracic outlet syndrome
C: SVC syndrome
D: Raynaud disease
column2:
Answer: C. SVC syndrome

Feedback:
Raynaud disease, TOS, Buerger disease and atherosclerosis can all lead to abnormally low digit pressures and abnormal PPG
tracing. SVC Syndrome refers to progressive thrombosis of the SVC that is usually related to adjacent malignant tumor
formation.

column1:
Which of the following non-stimulated PPG tracings is expected in a patient with Raynaud syndrome?

A: B
B: none of the tracings are consistent with Raynaud's syndrome
C: C
D: A
column2:
Answer: C. C

Feedback:
A PPG tracing from a non-stimulated Raynaud patient will have a slower than normal upstroke and delayed downslope BUT
there will be an early peak of the waveform.

column1:
The following pressure readings from a segmental pressure evaluation were obtained in a patient suffering from low back and thigh pain. What is the ABI for each leg? Lt Ankle 136 Rt Ankle 128 Lt Arm 124 Rt Arm 120

A: Rt = 1.03, Lt = 1.10
B: Rt = 1.03, Lt = 1.13
C: Rt = 0.94, Lt = 0.91
D: Rt = 1.07, Lt = 1.10
column2:
Answer: A. Rt = 1.03, Lt = 1.10

Feedback:
Rt ABI = 128/124 = 1.03
Lt ABI = 136/124 = 1.10
You should not have complicated math problems on the registry exam. The most important part of any calculation is to know
how to set up the calculation. The highest calf artery pressure is divided by the highest brachial pressure to calculate the ABI.

column1:
Which of the following is considered a limitation of the three cuff lower extremity segmental pressure exam?

A: The single thigh cuff normally overestimates the thigh pressure.
B: There is no way to differentiate lower thigh and popliteal arterial disease.
C: There is no way to differentiate proximal and distal calf artery disease.
D: There is no way to differentiate groin and ankle arterial disease.
column2:
Answer: B. There is no way to differentiate lower thigh and popliteal arterial disease.

Feedback:
Because there is only one cuff on the thigh, there is no way to differentiate disease in the lower thigh from disease in the
popliteal artery. The pressure is obtained mid-thigh and calf. The distal SFA and popliteal artery could have disease if the calf
pressure is decreased.

column1:
The ankle brachial index is commonly performed as a part of which of the following exams?

A: exercise testing
B: transcutaneous oximetry
C: photoplethysmography
D: strain gauge plethysmography
column2:
Answer: A. exercise testing

Feedback:
The ABI is performed at rest and with exercise. If the ABI decreases with exercise, arterial disease is present.

column1:
You are performing an upper extremity segmental pressure evaluation and obtain the following information: Brachial 140mmHg Upper forearm 132mmHg Wrist - Radial 108mmHg Wrist - Ulnar 130mmHg Which of the following correctly describes the findings?

A: obstruction of the distal brachial artery
B: subclavian steal
C: obstruction of the mid radial artery
D: obstruction of the mid ulnar artery
column2:
Answer: C. obstruction of the mid radial artery

Feedback:
The brachial and upper forearm pressure is normal, but there is a 22mmHg difference between the radial and ulnar pressures.
The vessel with the lower pressure has an obstruction proximal to the wrist.

column1:
Which of the following is a contraindication for a lower extremity segmental pressure exam?

A: ulceration on the distal calf
B: diabetes mellitus
C: ulceration on the 3rd and 4th toes
D: current anticoagulant treatment
column2:
Answer: A. ulceration on the distal calf

Feedback:
Contraindications:
Suspected or known acute deep venous thrombosis - use waveform analysis only or use of a toe/brachial index (TBI)
Recent surgery, trauma site or ulcers that cannot/should not be compressed by pressure cuffs
Patients with incompressible arteries due to medial calcification - TBI may be used instead
Morbidly obese patients - high-thigh pressure may be unobtainable or inaccurate due to limb girth
Patients with significant tremors or involuntary movements that may cause waveforms to be suboptimal or unreliable
Ulcerations on the 3rd and 4th toes would not be compressed by cuffs. While diabetes mellitus can cause medial calcification
which could inhibit the exam, not every patient with DM has medial calcification.

column1:
Which of the following is a normal response from the capillary beds of the feet and ankles after 5 minutes of vigorous exercise?

A: no change in the flow in normal vessels
B: vasodilation
C: vasoconstriction
D: increased osmotic pressures
column2:
Answer: B. vasodilation

Feedback:
Resistance decreases in the capillary beds to allow an increase in flow volume with exercise. Normal arteries do not
demonstrate any change in pressure with exercise.

column1:
Which of the following will increase the amplitude of a digital PPG tracing?

A: warming the patients foot and toes
B: increase sweep speed of the recorder
C: adding more gel between the skin and the sensor
D: adjust the baseline of the tracing
column2:
Answer: A. warming the patients foot and toes

Feedback:
PPG exams should be performed in a warm room. The digits can be warmed to cause vasodilation and increase pulse
amplitude. Because PPG measures blood volume, the amplitude increases with vasodilation and increased blood volume.. A
towel can be used to cover the digits and sensors to warm the digits and reduce effects of surrounding light. The sweep speed
effects the display of volume changes over time, not amplitude of the tracing. The baseline is used to indicate positive or
negative direction of flow. Changes in baseline are used to highlight flow patterns toward or away from the transducer.

column1:
Which extremity arterial evaluation technique can be described as the placement of multiple cuffs around the extremity that measure the volume changes beneath them?

A: Segmental Pressures
B: Pulse volume recording
C: Impedance plethysmography
D: Photoplethysmography
column2:
Answer: B. Pulse volume recording

Feedback:
Pulse volume recording is a type of plethysmography exam used to evaluate the arterial inflow of an extremity. Partially inflated
cuffs placed on the extremity at different levels record the volume changes on a strip chart recorder. The higher the amplitude
of the analog signal, the better the arterial inflow under that cuff.
Segmental pressure exams measure the pressure in the artery under the cuff.

column1:
A diabetic patient with a history of chronic renal failure and dialysis treatment presents with leg pain with walking. Which of the following is a potential complication for performing a segmental pressure exam on this patient?

A: The arm without the dialysis port is the only brachial pressure used in the evaluation.
B: Medial calcification may lead to overestimation of the ankle pressures.
C: Dialysis patients cannot be placed in the supine position for more than a few minutes, so the segmental exam will need to be performed with the patient seated in a chair.
D: More than one of the above
column2:
Answer: D. More than one of the above

Feedback:
The arm with the hemodialysis graft should never be used for taking systemic BP. Applying that much external pressure to the
arm can cause graft failure. Diabetes can lead to medial calcification, which causes overestimation of the ankle pressures.
Unless the patient has an additional health issue that prevents them from being supine for the exam, all patients having a
segmental pressure exam should be evaluated in the supine position.

column1:
In photoplethysmography, the sensor uses _________________ to detect ____________________ changes.

A: pressure cuff, blood pressure
B: light, blood volume
C: light, blood pressure
D: thin mercury tubing, volume
column2:
Answer: B. light, blood volume

Feedback:
The prefix photo- refers to light. Plethysmography is a method used to detect changes in blood volumes in the extremities.

column1:
A __________ cuff is usually used to assess wrist pressure and a ____________ cuff is usually used to assess digit pressure.

A: 7cm, 2.5cm
B: 2.5cm, 1cm
C: 12cm, 2.5cm
D: 10cm, 7cm
column2:
Answer: A. 7cm, 2.5cm

Feedback:
A 7cm cuff is usually used to assess wrist pressure and a 2.5cm cuff is usually used to assess digit pressure. Cuff size should be
20% larger than the diameter of the limb. The wrist is larger than the digits in diameter = larger cuff for wrist.

column1:
A patient presents with HTN, DM and left lower extremity pain that worsens as he walks. He usually begins to have pain after he walks to his mailbox with increasing pain as he returns to the house. The pain is relieved by resting his leg for a few minutes. A bilateral resting ABI is performed with the right leg, 1.12 and the left leg 1.34. Which of the following statements is true regarding the findings?

A: Blood pressure and PPG evaluation of the digital arteries should be performed.
B: These symptoms are most likely unrelated to arterial disease.
C: The patient should be evaluated for popliteal entrapment in the left leg.
D: Hypertensive disease usually leads to overestimation of the ABI values.
column2:
Answer: A. Blood pressure and PPG evaluation of the digital arteries should be performed.

Feedback:
Patients with diabetes have increased incidence of medial calcification of the extremity vessels which causes falsely increased
ABI measurements. An ABI over 1.30 usually indicates the presence of medial calcification. It is necessary to evaluate the digital
pressures for an accurate pressure index (Toe-Brachial Index) and PPG evaluation would also aid in flow characterization.

column1:
What setting on the photoplethysmography system should remain constant during an extremity arterial evaluation and if it is adjusted it must be documented on the tracing?

A: Signal output
B: Sweep speed
C: Size control
D: Baseline level
column2:
Answer: C. Size control

Feedback:
The size control on the PPG system should be set at a constant level (10) and should not be changed during the exam. The PPG
tracings demonstrate the amplitude of the reflected light, which indicates blood volume changes in the toe. The size setting
should be kept constant to best demonstrate changes in blood volume between toes/feet. If the size control is adjusted, it must
be documented on the affected tracings.

column1:
You are evaluating a patient with suspected Raynaud disease using PPG. After obtaining a baseline tracing, you place the hand in cold water for 2 minutes. Once the hand is removed, PPG sensors are reapplied to the fingertips. Which of the following correctly describes normal results for this exam?

A: waveforms return to baseline within 7 minutes after removing the hand from the cold water
B: waveforms return to baseline within 5 minutes after removing the hand from the cold water
C: waveform amplitude increases to at least 4X the original
D: waveform amplitude increases to at least 2X the original
column2:
Answer: B. waveforms return to baseline within 5 minutes after removing the hand from the cold water

Feedback:
Cold sensitivity testing is used to evaluate suspected Raynaud disease. The cold water elicits vasoconstriction. A normal exam
would demonstrate PPG tracings that return to baseline levels within 5 minutes after the hand is removed from the cold water.
An abnormal exam would have a delayed return to baseline readings.

column1:
The lower extremity segmental pressure exam is most commonly performed with a/an ___________________ to obtain the Doppler signal for the pressure measurements at the ankle.

A: 8-10Mhz vector array
B: 5-7MHz linear array
C: 8-10MHz CW Doppler probe
D: 2-3MHz CW Doppler probe
column2:
Answer: C. 8-10MHz CW Doppler probe

Feedback:
The dedicated CW probe is used to obtain the Doppler signal at the ankle while pressure measurements are obtained in the leg.

column1:
Which of the following is a contraindication for exercise testing?

A: Non-compressible calf arteries
B: Systemic HTN with systolic pressure >200mmHg
C: History of cardiac procedure or myocardial infarction
D: More than one of the above
column2:
Answer: D. More than one of the above

Feedback:
Contraindications for Exercise Testing:
Known disease in one extremity that is untreated
Inability to walk without assistance
History of stroke with residual paralysis
Shortness of breath
Low exercise tolerance
Systemic HTN with systolic pressure >200mmHg
Noncompressible calf arteries
Unstable angina and/or use of nitroglycerin for chest pain
History of cardiac procedure or myocardial infarction - must be cleared for exercise testing by cardiologist

column1:
The image demonstrates what type of vascular evaluation technique?

A: Pulse Volume Recording
B: Segmental pressures
C: Oculopneumoplethysmography
D: Analog Doppler
column2:
Answer: A. Pulse Volume Recording

Feedback:
Pulse volume recording is a type of arterial plethysmography. Multiple cuffs are placed on the extremity at different levels. The
cuffs are inflated to 55-60mmHg and the arterial pulsations under each cuff are recorded as an analog waveform. The greater
the amplitude of the waveform and the better the circulation.
The recordings displayed indicate a PVR exam that is positive for thoracic outlet syndrome. Note the high amplitude of the flow
under the cuff with the arm in the normal or rest position. The signal becomes very low amplitude while the patient maneuver's
their arm. These abnormal waveforms indicate the presence of a functional obstruction causing little volume change under the
cuff.

column1:
Which of the following could cause a false reduction in ankle pressure on an average sized patient?

A: using the 12cm cuff for the ankle
B: medial calcification of the calf vessels
C: cuff is loosely wrapped around the arm
D: evaluating the pressure with the patient seated in a chair
column2:
Answer: A. using the 12cm cuff for the ankle

Feedback:
False elevation of the ankle pressure = medial calcification, cuff too loose, cuff too small, patient not supine (hydrostatic
pressure)
False reduction in the ankle pressure = cuff too large, congestive heart failure, coarctation

column1:
Increased peripheral resistance will lead to______________ amplitude in PVR tracings.

A: increased
B: decreased
C: little change in
D: the formation of a second dicrotic notch
column2:
Answer: A. increased

Feedback:
Increased peripheral resistance will lead to increased amplitude in PVR tracings. Proximal disease will lead to a reduced
amplitude and loss of the dicrotic notch.

column1:
The PVR tracings from a lower extremity arterial exam. Which of the following explains these findings?

A: unable to determine with PVR tracing from the calf
B: stenosis proximal to the proximal thigh cuff
C: stenosis distal to the proximal thigh cuff
D: stenosis distal to the distal thigh cuff
column2:
Answer: C. stenosis distal to the proximal thigh cuff

Feedback:
When a PVR tracing is reduced in amplitude, demonstrates a rounded peak and absence of the dicrotic notch, stenosis proximal
to the cuff is suspected.

column1:
Venous PPG tracings are obtained. The right side demonstrates a venous refill time of 30 seconds. The left side demonstrates a refill time of 23 seconds. Which of the following describes the next step in evaluation?

A: A tourniquet will be applied to the left leg at the thigh level and the VRT assessment will be repeated.
B: A tourniquet will be applied to the right leg at the thigh level and the VRT assessment will be repeated.
C: A tourniquet will be applied to both legs at the thigh level and the VRT assessment will be repeated.
D: The results are normal. No further evaluation is necessary.
column2:
Answer: D. The results are normal. No further evaluation is necessary.

Feedback:
A normal VRT is greater than 20 seconds.

column1:
Upon cessation of exercise, the ankle pressures in a normal patient will:

A: increase to normal levels in less than 10 minutes
B: decrease to normal levels in less than 10 minutes
C: increase to normal levels in less than 5 minutes
D: decrease to normal levels in less than 5 minutes
column2:
Answer: D. decrease to normal levels in less than 5 minutes

Feedback:
Upon cessation of exercise, the ankle pressures in a normal patient will decrease to normal levels within 5 minutes. Ankle and
arm pressures should show a mild increase with exercise. Because they increase at relatively the same levels, the ABI usually
stays the same or increases slightly.

column1:
Which of the following is NOT an appropriate reason to terminate the treadmill exercise for a lower extremity arterial exam?

A: patient complains of leg pain
B: patient complains of shortness of breath
C: patient complains that the treadmill is too noisy
D: patient complains of chest pain
column2:
Answer: C. patient complains that the treadmill is too noisy

Feedback:
Anytime the patient reports symptoms of pain in the legs or the chest, the exam should be terminated. Never try to talk the
patient into continuing the exam. Always record the duration of the exercise, location of the symptoms and changes in ankle
pressures.

column1:
The following pressure readings from a segmental pressure evaluation were obtained in a patient suffering from low back and thigh pain when walking. Which of the following describes a possible reason for the patient's symptoms? Lt Ankle 136 Rt Ankle 128 Lt Arm 124 Rt Arm 120

A: neurogenic claudication
B: stenosis of the external iliac artery
C: stenosis of the common femoral artery
D: May Thurner syndrome
column2:
Answer: A. neurogenic claudication

Feedback:
When lower extremity symptoms are caused by ambulation and the ABI exam is normal, the symptoms are called
pseudoclaudication or neurogenic claudication. The most common cause of pseudoclaudication due to lumbar spinal stenosis, a
condition that occurs when the spaces narrow between the vertebrae in your lower back.

column1:
Which of the following exams does not use PW or CW Doppler?

A: periorbital exam
B: transcranial exam
C: extracranial exam
D: pulse volume recording
column2:
Answer: D. pulse volume recording

Feedback:
Pulse volume recording is performed using blood pressure cuffs to measure volume changes under the cuffs.

column1:
A diabetic patient presents with an injury to his big toe that won't seem to heal and has been open for over 3 weeks. Which of the following exam techniques should be used to evaluate this patient?

A: treadmill testing
B: segmental pressure exam
C: cold sensitivity testing
D: digital pressures and PPG evaluation
column2:
Answer: D. digital pressures and PPG evaluation

Feedback:
Digital pressures are more accurate in evaluating flow in the legs/feet than the ABI due to medial calcification of the calf/ankle
vessels.

column1:
When the Allen test is performed with significant abnormal findings, what happens to the digit PPG tracings with radial artery compression?

A: amplitude significantly decreases
B: dicrotic notch moves to the top of the peak amplitude
C: amplitude increases by at least 50%
D: amplitude increases by at least 4 times the original amplitude
column2:
Answer: A. amplitude significantly decreases

Feedback:
A normal Allen test involves clenching the hands and raising the arms to drain the blood from the hands, compression of the
radial and ulnar artery is performed and the hand should turn pale. Release compression of the radial artery and a normal
ulnar artery will allow the hand to return to normal color with normal flow in the ulnar artery. This process is repeated with
releasing ulnar artery compression and maintaining radial artery compression.
If the Allen test provides abnormal results, when the compressed artery is released there is no return to normal color because
the uncompressed artery is obstructed. The palmar arch is not patent because the uncompressed artery is obstructed. This test
is important for evaluating arm circulation prior to the establishment of a hemodialysis access graft.
PPG sensors can be placed on the digits for a recordable response to the Allen Test. If the ulnar and radial arteries are
compressed, the PPG tracings should be very low amplitude. When compression of the radial artery is released, the PPG
waveform should increase in amplitude. If it remains low and flat, the radial artery is obstructed.

column1:
A patient presents with a script for an upper extremity PPG exam to rule out Raynaud syndrome. How will you perform the exam?

A: Raynaud syndrome cannot be evaluated with PPG techniques.
B: Place PPG sensors on the tips of the fingers before and after cold sensitivity testing.
C: Place the PPG sensors at the antecubital fossa and apply tourniquets at multiple levels on the arm to assess changes in flow.
D: Place PPG sensors on the wrists before and after cold sensitivity testing.
column2:
Answer: B. Place PPG sensors on the tips of the fingers before and after cold sensitivity testing.

Feedback:
PPG sensors are used to detect changes in flow in the finger tips during cold sensitivity testing for Raynaud syndrome.

column1:
Which of the following indicates single level arterial obstruction in the leg?

A: ABI >0.5
B: velocity of 200cm/s or less
C: post exercise ankle recovery time 6-12 minutes
D: ABI <0.5
column2:
Answer: A. ABI >0.5

Feedback:
ABI >0.5 single level disease; ABI <0.5 multi-level disease

column1:
Which of the following is not a characteristic of a normal PVR tracing of the lower extremity?

A: dicrotic notch
B: diastolic flow reversal
C: peaked amplitude
D: anacrotic limb
column2:
Answer: B. diastolic flow reversal

Feedback:
PVR waveforms do not have a flow reversal component. The tracing represents volume which never goes into the negative.

column1:
